title: Introduction to midnight: Core Modeling and Fast Solvers
text: midnight fully integrates tidymodels ecosystem. mid_reg() function allows specify models using familiar parsnip syntax, enabling pipeline-based workflows model fitting hyperparameter tuning.
code: library(midr) library(midnight) library(parsnip) library(ggplot2) library(patchwork)  # fitting a model with interaction using mid_reg() mid <- mid_reg(   params_main = 50, # k   penalty = 5 # lambda ) |>   fit(Ozone ~ (Wind + Solar.R) ^ 2, na.omit(airquality))  mid #> parsnip model object #>  #>  #> Call: #> interpret(formula = Ozone ~ (Wind + Solar.R)^2, data = data, #>  k = 50, lambda = 5) #>  #> Intercept: 42.099 #>  #> Main Effects: #> 2 main effect terms #>  #> Interactions: #> 1 interaction term #>  #> Uninterpreted Variation Ratio: 0.33087

title: Introduction to midnight: Core Modeling and Fast Solvers
text: situations requiring fitting surrogate model multivariate responses, midnight provides highly optimized OLS solvers. can compare standard RcppEigen implementations matrix-based solvers provided midnight. Furthermore, can globally override default Least Squares Solvers used mid_reg() (interpret()) leverage speed boost across entire modeling pipeline using nightfall().
code: # standard RcppEigen solver fit1 <- RcppEigen::fastLm(   Ozone ~ Wind + Solar.R, na.omit(airquality) ) print(as.matrix(fit1$coefficients)) #>                   [,1] #> (Intercept) 77.2460424 #> Wind        -5.4017973 #> Solar.R      0.1003506 fit2 <- RcppEigen::fastLm(   Temp ~ Wind + Solar.R, na.omit(airquality) ) # fastLmMatrix print(as.matrix(fit2$coefficients)) #>                    [,1] #> (Intercept) 85.70227521 #> Wind        -1.25187025 #> Solar.R      0.02453254 fit3 <- fastLmMatrix(   cbind(Ozone, Temp) ~ Wind + Solar.R, na.omit(airquality) ) print(fit3$coefficients) #>                  Ozone        Temp #> (Intercept) 77.2460424 85.70227521 #> Wind        -5.4017973 -1.25187025 #> Solar.R      0.1003506  0.02453254 # activate midnight package nightfall()  mids <- mid_reg(   params_main = 50, # k   penalty = 5 # lambda ) |>   fit(cbind(Ozone, Temp) ~ (Wind + Solar.R) ^ 2, airquality) #> custom solver 'qr' is used  mids #> parsnip model object #>  #>  #> Call: #> interpret(formula = cbind(Ozone, Temp) ~ (Wind + Solar.R)^2, #>  data = data, k = 50, lambda = 5) #>  #> Intercept: 42.099, 77.793 #>  #> Main Effects: #> 2 main effect terms #>  #> Interactions: #> 1 interaction term #>  #> Uninterpreted Variation Ratio: 0.33087, 0.52745

title: Introduction to midnight: Core Modeling and Fast Solvers
text: understand output mid_reg(), midnight provides S3 methods MID models like persp.mid() ggmid.midimp(), activated nightfall() called. methods help translate complex model outputs interpretable insights.

title: Introduction to midnight: Core Modeling and Fast Solvers
text: Using ggmid(type = 'beeswarm'), well 'violinplot' 'sinaplot', can effortlessly plot feature importance distributions understand impact individual variables interactions.
code: imp <- mid.importance(mid$fit, data = airquality) p1 <- ggmid(imp, type = "beeswarm", theme = "Hokusai3") +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") p2 <- ggmid(imp, type = "violin", theme = "moon") +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") p1 + p2

title: Introduction to midnight: Core Modeling and Fast Solvers
text: S3 method persp() “mid” creates surface plots, making easy examine joint effect two predictors.

title: Introduction to midnight: Core Modeling and Fast Solvers
text: ensure visualizations accurate aesthetically pleasing, midnight adds wide array color palettes colormap, DALEX MetBrewer well three original themes.

title: Introduction to midnight: Core Modeling and Fast Solvers
text: midnight package combines computational speed fastLmMatrix() modern API tidymodels via mid_reg(). paired powerful visualization color theme systems, provides comprehensive toolkit building, scaling, interpreting sophisticated models.

what: Parsnip Engine for Maximum Interpretation Decomposition Models
title: Parsnip Engine for Maximum Interpretation Decomposition Models
text: ‘midnight’ package implements ‘parsnip’ engine ‘midr’ package, allowing users seamlessly fit, tune, evaluate MID (Maximum Interpretation Decomposition) models ‘tidymodels’ workflows. Development augmentation package driven research “Moonlight Seminar 2025”, collaborative study group actuaries Institute Actuaries Japan focused advancing practical applications interpretable models.

title: Parsnip Engine for Maximum Interpretation Decomposition Models
text: can install development version midnight GitHub :

title: Parsnip Engine for Maximum Interpretation Decomposition Models
text: basic example shows solve common problem:
code: library(tidymodels) library(midr) library(midnight) library(gridExtra) library(ISLR2)  options(   midr.sequential = "moonlit" ) # split dataset into training / validating subsets set.seed(42) usecol <- c("mnth", "hr", "workingday", "weathersit",             "temp", "hum", "windspeed", "bikers") all <- ISLR2::Bikeshare |>   select(all_of(usecol)) |>   mutate(workingday = as.factor(workingday)) holdout <- initial_split(all, prop = .5) train <- training(holdout) valid <- testing(holdout) # create a first-order mid surrogate model mid_spec_1 <- mid_reg() mid_spec_1 #> mid reg Model Specification (regression) #>  #> Computational engine: midr # fit the model mid_1 <- mid_spec_1 %>%   fit(bikers ~ ., train) mid_1 #> parsnip model object #>  #>  #> Call: #> interpret(formula = bikers ~ ., data = data) #>  #> Intercept: 146.06 #>  #> Main Effects: #> 7 main effect terms #>  #> Uninterpreted Variation Ratio: 0.30041 # evaluate the model augment(mid_1, new_data = valid) %>%   rmse(truth = bikers, estimate = .pred) #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>   .metric .estimator .estimate #>   <chr>   <chr>          <dbl> #> 1 rmse    standard        72.9 imp_1 <- mid.importance(mid_1$fit, data = train) grid.arrange(nrow = 2,  ggmid(imp_1),  ggmid(mid_1$fit, "hr"),  ggmid(mid_1$fit, "temp"),  ggmid(mid_1$fit, "mnth") ) # create a second-order mid surrogate model via "custom formula" mid_spec_2 <- mid_reg(penalty = 0.000001) mid_spec_2 #> mid reg Model Specification (regression) #>  #> Main Arguments: #>   penalty = 1e-06 #>  #> Computational engine: midr # fit the model mid_2 <- mid_spec_2 %>%   fit(bikers ~ .^2, train) # pass original data on to interpret() mid_2 #> parsnip model object #>  #>  #> Call: #> interpret(formula = bikers ~ .^2, data = data, lambda = 1e-06) #>  #> Intercept: 146.06 #>  #> Main Effects: #> 7 main effect terms #>  #> Interactions: #> 21 interaction terms #>  #> Uninterpreted Variation Ratio: 0.069436 # evaluate the model augment(mid_2, new_data = valid) %>%   rmse(truth = bikers, estimate = .pred) #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>   .metric .estimator .estimate #>   <chr>   <chr>          <dbl> #> 1 rmse    standard        44.3 imp_2 <- mid.importance(mid_2$fit, data = train) grid.arrange(nrow = 2,  ggmid(imp_2, max.nterms = 15),  ggmid(mid_2$fit, "hr"),  ggmid(mid_2$fit, "temp"),  ggmid(mid_2$fit, "hr:workingday", type = "data",        data = valid, main.effects = TRUE) ) par.midr(mar = c(1, 1, 0, 0)) persp(mid_2$fit, "temp:hr", theta = 50, phi = 20, shade = .5)

title: Parsnip Engine for Maximum Interpretation Decomposition Models
code: # create a second-order mid surrogate model via "custom formula" #  mid_spec_3 <- mid_reg(   params_main = tune(),   params_inter = tune(),   penalty = tune(),   terms = ~(mnth+hr+workingday+weathersit+temp+hum+windspeed)^2   # all main effects and all 2-way interactions ) |>   set_engine(     engine = "midr",     method = "llt",     verbosity = 0L   ) mid_spec_3 #> mid reg Model Specification (regression) #>  #> Main Arguments: #>   penalty = tune() #>   params_main = tune() #>   params_inter = tune() #>   terms = ~(mnth + hr + workingday + weathersit + temp + hum + windspeed)^2 #>  #> Engine-Specific Arguments: #>   method = llt #>   verbosity = 0 #>  #> Computational engine: midr # define a cross validation method set.seed(42) cv <- vfold_cv(train, v = 2) # execute the hyperparameter tuning tune_res <- mid_spec_3 %>%   tune_bayes(     bikers ~ ., # not allow tune_bayes() to preprocess data     resamples = cv,     iter = 50   ) tune_best <- select_best(tune_res, metric = "rmse") tune_best #> # A tibble: 1 × 4 #>   penalty params_main params_inter .config #>     <dbl>       <int>        <int> <chr>   #> 1   0.668          70            5 Iter23 # create a second-order mid surrogate model via "custom formula" mid_spec_4 <- mid_reg(   params_main = tune_best$params_main,   params_inter = tune_best$params_inter,   penalty = tune_best$penalty ) mid_spec_4 #> mid reg Model Specification (regression) #>  #> Main Arguments: #>   penalty = tune_best$penalty #>   params_main = tune_best$params_main #>   params_inter = tune_best$params_inter #>  #> Computational engine: midr # fit the model mid_tune <- mid_spec_4 %>%   fit(bikers ~ .^2, train) # pass original data on to interpret() mid_tune #> parsnip model object #>  #>  #> Call: #> interpret(formula = bikers ~ .^2, data = data, k = 70L, lambda = 0.668346758138943, #>  k2 = 5L) #>  #> Intercept: 146.06 #>  #> Main Effects: #> 7 main effect terms #>  #> Interactions: #> 21 interaction terms #>  #> Uninterpreted Variation Ratio: 0.080807 # evaluate the model augment(mid_tune, new_data = valid) %>%   rmse(truth = bikers, estimate = .pred) #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>   .metric .estimator .estimate #>   <chr>   <chr>          <dbl> #> 1 rmse    standard        43.0 imp_tune <- mid.importance(mid_tune$fit, data = train) grid.arrange(nrow = 2,  ggmid(imp_tune, max.nterms = 15),  ggmid(mid_tune$fit, "hr"),  ggmid(mid_tune$fit, "temp"),  ggmid(mid_tune$fit, "hr:workingday", type = "data",        data = valid, main.effects = TRUE) ) par.midr(mar = c(1, 1, 0, 0)) persp(mid_tune$fit, "temp:hr", theta = 50, phi = 20, shade = .5)

title: Fit Multivariate Linear Models — fastLmMatrix
text: fastLmMatrix() estimates linear model multivariate response using one several methods implemented using Eigen linear algebra library.

title: Fit Multivariate Linear Models — fastLmMatrix
code: fastLmMatrix(x, ...)  # Default S3 method fastLmMatrix(x, y, tol = 1e-07, method = 0L, ...)  # S3 method for class 'formula' fastLmMatrix(formula, data = list(), method = 0L, ...)

title: Fit Multivariate Linear Models — fastLmMatrix
text: x model matrix \(X\). ... optional parameters passed methods. y response matrix \(Y\). tol tolerance rank calculation. method integer value 0 column-pivoted QR decomposition, 1 unpivoted QR decomposition, 2 LLT Cholesky, 3 LDLT Cholesky, 4 Jacobi singular value decomposition (SVD). Default zero. formula object class "formula" (one can coerced class): symbolic description model fitted. data optional data frame, list environment (object coercible .data.frame data frame) containing variables model.

title: Fit Multivariate Linear Models — fastLmMatrix
text: fastLmMatrix() returns list following components: coefficients \(p \times k\) matrix coefficients. fitted.values \(n \times k\) matrix fitted values. residuals \(n \times k\) matrix residuals. rank integer giving numeric rank model matrix \(X\).

title: Fit Multivariate Linear Models — fastLmMatrix
text: fastLmMatrix() performance-optimized version standard lm function, specifically designed handle multivariate responses (\(Y\) matrix). leverages Eigen C++ template library high-performance linear algebra, utilizing Eigen::Map avoid unnecessary memory copies providing several decomposition methods different trade-offs speed numerical stability.

title: Fit Multivariate Linear Models — fastLmMatrix
code: # Multivariate Regression with Anscombe's Quartet # Regress three 'y' variables on 'x1' simultaneously Y <- as.matrix(anscombe[, c("y1", "y2", "y3")]) X <- as.matrix(cbind(1, anscombe$x1)) # Include intercept  fit_ans <- fastLmMatrix(X, Y, method = 0L) print(fit_ans$coefficients) #>             y1       y2        y3 #> [1,] 3.0000909 3.000909 3.0024545 #> [2,] 0.5000909 0.500000 0.4997273  # Formula interface with cbind() fit_form <- fastLmMatrix(cbind(y1, y2, y3) ~ x1, data = anscombe) print(fit_form$coefficients) #>                    y1       y2        y3 #> (Intercept) 3.0000909 3.000909 3.0024545 #> x1          0.5000909 0.500000 0.4997273

what: Plot MID Importance with ggplot2 — ggmid.midimp
title: Plot MID Importance with ggplot2 — ggmid.midimp
text: midnight package extends midr::ggmid() provide modern distribution plots MID feature importance. Added types include sina, beeswarm, violin plots.

title: Plot MID Importance with ggplot2 — ggmid.midimp
code: # S3 method for class 'midimp' ggmid(object, type = NULL, theme = NULL, terms = NULL, max.nterms = 30, ...)

title: Plot MID Importance with ggplot2 — ggmid.midimp
text: object "midimp" object visualized. type plotting style. addition standard types ("barplot", "boxplot", "dotchart", "heatmap"), extended method supports "violinplot", "sinaplot", "beeswarm". theme character string object defining color theme. See color.theme details. terms optional character vector specifying terms display. max.nterms integer specifying maximum number terms display. Defaults 30. ... optional parameters passed layers.

title: Plot MID Importance with ggplot2 — ggmid.midimp
text: ggmid.midimp() returns "ggplot" object.

title: Plot MID Importance with ggplot2 — ggmid.midimp
text: S3 method midr::ggmid() generic "midimp" objects created midr::mid.importance(). method replaces primary layer modern distribution geoms type one extended options.

title: Plot MID Importance with ggplot2 — ggmid.midimp
text: S3 method registered automatically midnight package loaded, activated nightfall() explicitly called.

title: Plot MID Importance with ggplot2 — ggmid.midimp
code: library(midr) library(midnight)  mid <- interpret(Ozone ~ .^2, airquality, lambda = .5) #> 'model' not passed: response variable in 'data' is used imp <- mid.importance(mid)  # Activate the richer S3 method old <- nightfall()  # Create a violin plot ggmid(imp, type = "violinplot", theme = "moon")   # Sina and Beeswarm plots require additional packages if (requireNamespace("ggforce", quietly = TRUE)) {   ggmid(imp, type = "sinaplot", theme = "bicolor") }   if (requireNamespace("ggbeeswarm", quietly = TRUE)) {   ggmid(imp, type = "beeswarm", theme = "Hokusai3") }   # Restore the options daybreak() options(old)

title: MID Regression — mid_reg
text: mid_reg() defines model can predict numeric values predictors using set functions two predictors. function fits regression model based Maximum Interpretation Decomposition.

title: MID Regression — mid_reg
code: mid_reg(   mode = "regression",   engine = "midr",   model = NULL,   params_main = NULL,   params_inter = NULL,   penalty = NULL,   terms = NULL )  fit_mid_reg(   formula,   data,   weights = NULL,   model = NULL,   params_main = NULL,   params_inter = NULL,   penalty = NULL,   terms = NULL,   ... )

title: MID Regression — mid_reg
text: mode single character string type model. Currently, "regression" supported. engine single character string specifying computational engine use. default "midr". model optional fitted model object (black-box model) interpreted. Default NULL. params_main integer specifying maximum number sample points (knots) model main effects. Corresponds argument k[1] midr::interpret(). params_inter integer specifying maximum number sample points (knots) model interaction effects. Corresponds argument k[2] k2 midr::interpret(). penalty non-negative number representing total amount regularization. Corresponds argument lambda midr::interpret(). terms character vector formula term labels included fitting process. formula object class formula: symbolic description model fitted. data data frame containing variables model. weights optional vector case weights. ... arguments passed midr::interpret().

title: MID Regression — mid_reg
text: "model_spec" object class "mid_reg".

title: MID Regression — mid_reg
text: function main specification parsnip model. underlying fitting performed fit_mid_reg(), helper function connects fit() midr::interpret() function.

what: midnight: Parsnip Engine for Maximum Interpretation Decomposition Models — midnight-package
title: midnight: Parsnip Engine for Maximum Interpretation Decomposition Models — midnight-package
text: Provides 'parsnip' engine 'midr' package, enabling users fit, tune, evaluate Maximum Interpretation Decomposition (MID) models within 'tidymodels' framework. Developed research 'Moonlight Seminar 2025', study group actuaries Institute Actuaries Japan, enhance practical applications interpretable modeling. Detailed methodology available Asashiba et al. (2025) doi:10.48550/arXiv.2506.08338 .

title: midnight: Parsnip Engine for Maximum Interpretation Decomposition Models — midnight-package
text: Maintainer: Ryoichi Asashiba ryoichi.asashiba@gmail.com

what: Generate Synthetic Data using Mixup — mixup
title: Generate Synthetic Data using Mixup — mixup
text: mixup() generates synthetic data points combining pairs existing observations. numeric variables, calculates convex combination. categorical variables (factors characters), randomly selects one two values based generated combination weight.

title: Generate Synthetic Data using Mixup — mixup
code: mixup(object, n = nrow(object), weights = NULL, alpha = 0.2, seed = NULL)

title: Generate Synthetic Data using Mixup — mixup
text: object data frame matrix containing original data. n integer specifying number synthetic observations generate. Defaults number observations. weights optional numeric vector probability weights sampling observations. alpha numeric scalar representing shape parameter Beta distribution. Smaller values (e.g., 0.2) generate data closer original points (acting local perturbation). Defaults 0.2. seed optional integer set random seed reproducibility.

title: Generate Synthetic Data using Mixup — mixup
text: mixup() returns object class object (either "matrix" "data.frame") containing n generated synthetic observations.

title: Generate Synthetic Data using Mixup — mixup
text: Mixup algorithm creates new synthetic observation \(x_{new}\) two randomly sampled observations \(x_1\) \(x_2\). combination weight \(\lambda\) drawn Beta distribution, \(\text{Beta}(\alpha, \alpha)\). numeric variables, synthetic data generated via convex combination: $$x_{new} = \lambda x_1 + (1 - \lambda) x_2$$ non-numeric variables, value chosen \(x_1\) probability \(\lambda\), \(x_2\) probability \(1 - \lambda\).

title: Generate Synthetic Data using Mixup — mixup
code: library(midr) library(midnight)  # Fit a model fit <- lm(Volume ~ I(Girth^2) + Girth + Height, trees)  # Generate synthetic rows and combine it with the original data mixup_trees <- mixup(trees, 500) summary(mixup_trees) #>      Girth           Height          Volume      #>  Min.   : 8.30   Min.   :63.00   Min.   :10.20   #>  1st Qu.:11.11   1st Qu.:72.00   1st Qu.:19.90   #>  Median :12.90   Median :76.00   Median :24.60   #>  Mean   :13.20   Mean   :75.79   Mean   :30.03   #>  3rd Qu.:14.90   3rd Qu.:80.00   3rd Qu.:37.53   #>  Max.   :20.60   Max.   :87.00   Max.   :77.00   combined_trees <- rbind(trees, mixup_trees)  # Fit MID models mid1 <- interpret(Volume ~ ., trees, fit, k = 25, ok = TRUE) #> singular fit encountered mid2 <- interpret(Volume ~ ., combined_trees, fit, ok = TRUE) mid3 <- interpret(Volume ~ ., trees, fit, lambda = .1, ok = TRUE)  # Compare main effects ml <- midlist(singular = mid1, mixup = mid2, penalty = mid3) ggmid(ml, "Girth", linewidth = 3/4)  ggmid(ml, "Height", linewidth = 3/4)

what: Transition into and out of Midnight — nightfall
title: Transition into and out of Midnight — nightfall
text: nightfall() activates extended features provided midnight package. registers customized S3 methods (e.g., ggmid.midimp()), switches underlying solvers highly optimized Eigen-based routines, applies midnight-themed color palettes. daybreak() reverses changes, restoring default behavior, solvers, themes midr package.

title: Transition into and out of Midnight — nightfall
code: nightfall(methods = TRUE, solvers = TRUE, themes = TRUE)  daybreak(methods = TRUE, solvers = TRUE, themes = TRUE)

title: Transition into and out of Midnight — nightfall
text: methods logical. TRUE, registers (restores) extended S3 methods. solvers logical. TRUE, switches (restores) calculation solvers matrix responses. themes logical. TRUE, applies (restores) specific color themes.

title: Transition into and out of Midnight — nightfall
text: nightfall() daybreak() return invisible list containing previous options solvers themes.

what: Perspective Plot of MID Effects — persp.mid
title: Perspective Plot of MID Effects — persp.mid
text: Visualizes combined effect two variables "mid" object using 3D perspective plot.

title: Perspective Plot of MID Effects — persp.mid
code: # S3 method for class 'mid' persp(object, xvar, yvar = NULL, ..., xval = NULL, yval = NULL)

title: Perspective Plot of MID Effects — persp.mid
text: object "mid" object, typically result midr::interpret(). xvar character string name variable x-axis. Alternatively, single string format xvar:yvar can provided, case yvar can omitted. yvar character string name variable y-axis. ... additional arguments passed graphics:::persp.default(). Used customize plot's appearance, view angles (theta, phi) color (col). xval numeric character vector specifying sequence values x-axis. yval numeric character vector specifying sequence values y-axis.

title: Perspective Plot of MID Effects — persp.mid
text: persp.mid() invisibly returns viewing transformation matrix, see persp details. function primarily called side effect creating plot.

title: Perspective Plot of MID Effects — persp.mid
text: S3 method persp() generic calculates sum main effects xvar yvar interaction effect (xvar:yvar). resulting sum plotted height z-axis.

title: Perspective Plot of MID Effects — persp.mid
code: mid <- midr::interpret(mpg ~ wt * hp + factor(am), data = mtcars, lambda = .5) #> 'model' not passed: response variable in 'data' is used  # Create a basic perspective plot persp(mid, xvar = "wt", yvar = "hp")   # Customize the plot by passing arguments to graphics:::persp.default() persp(mid, "wt", "hp", theta = 210, phi = 20, col = "lightblue", shade = .5)  persp(mid, "factor(am):wt", theta = 210, shade = .2)

title: midnight (development version) 0.1.1.90x
text: Added nightfall() daybreak() functions registering S3 method, setting default OLS solvers color themes via options. Added mixup() creating synthetic data using Mixup algorithm. Reduced dependencies packages. Added fastLmMatrix(), wrapper function RcppEigen::fastLm() supporting matrix responses. Renamed Updated ggmid.midimp wrapper (better alignment midr 0.6.0).

title: midnight 0.1.0
text: Added wrapper method ggmid.mid.importance new types: “violin”, “beeswarm”, “sinaplot”. Renamed main functions (mid_surrogate() mid_reg()). Added persp.mid(). Added “moon” “moonlit” color themes Added package document. Updated wrapper fit function fit_mid_mid() improve memory efficiency. Opened pkgdown website added favicons. Added mid_surrogate() make_mid_mid(). Created package hex logo.
